title: Telegram frontend: long-polling bot + whitelist + command→hub mapping
description: A thin presentation adapter (new src/telegram/ module) that exposes the existing agent-bober hub, task inbox, approve/steer gate, calendar plans, and medical ingest over a locally-run, long-polling (getUpdates) Telegram bot. It adds no new domain logic: plain text routes to task capture, slash commands route to scoped prioritization, inline buttons route to the existing approval markers, and document uploads route (per-upload opt-in) to medical ingest. A single outbound funnel enforces the control-plane privacy boundary so the non-end-to-end bot carries only summaries and notifications, never raw PHI or financial detail.

approvalEvidence: package.json deps (execa present; no Telegram library present)
.bober/research/research-20260628-telegram-features-and-topology.md (validated Tier-1 topology; Bot API 10.0 bot-to-bot + Secretary Mode; 13/25 claims confirmed by 3-vote verification)
src/fleet/synthesis.ts:15-39 (SynthesisBundle { rounds, childResults, findings } — the artifact feat-9 reads)

features.title: Local long-polling Telegram bot process + CLI command
features.description: A new `agent-bober telegram` CLI command starts a locally-run bot that polls Telegram via getUpdates. No web server, no public URL, no open inbound port. Each person runs their own bot against their own agent-bober checkout.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: `agent-bober telegram` is registered in src/cli/index.ts and reachable via `agent-bober telegram --help`.
AC2: The bot reads its token from TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and connects using long-polling getUpdates (no webhook, no listening socket).
AC3: Starting with a missing/empty TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN exits non-zero with a message naming the missing variable.

features.title: User-ID whitelist authorization
features.description: Only Telegram accounts whose numeric id appears in TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS (comma-separated) may interact. Any other account receives an access-denied reply that echoes its own numeric id, and its message is otherwise ignored.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: A sender id present in TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS is admitted; a sender id absent from it is denied.
AC2: The denial reply string contains the exact numeric id of the denied sender.
AC3: A denied sender's message triggers no capture, command, approval, upload, or ingest side effect.

features.title: Control-plane outbound privacy funnel
features.description: Every outbound bot message passes through a single sendSafe funnel that enforces the control-plane boundary: the bot carries summaries, titles, and notifications only — never raw PHI or financial detail. Handlers produce content; only the funnel calls the Telegram send transport.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: All bot replies in the codebase are emitted via the sendSafe funnel; no handler calls the Telegram send transport directly.
AC2: The funnel is the single chokepoint later sprints extend for streaming and silent delivery.

features.title: Plain-text → task inbox capture
features.description: Any non-command text from a whitelisted user is captured as a new open task in the shared inbox pool (spec-20260628-task-inbox) with the message text as its title and no other required fields. The bot replies with a one-line confirmation summary.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: Sending arbitrary free text creates exactly one inbox task whose title equals the message text.
AC2: Capture never blocks on a missing field (no due date / domain prompt); unknowns are left for a later triage pass.
AC3: Messages beginning with '/' are not captured as tasks — they fall through to the command router.

features.title: Scoped prioritization commands (/today, /priority, /decide X vs Y)
features.description: Slash commands invoke the priority hub (spec-20260628-priority-hub) with an ephemeral, per-question scope parsed from the command text, and reply with a numbered ranked summary. /decide X vs Y produces decision-mode scope filtered to X and Y; /today and /priority produce a time-horizon or general scope.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: /decide X vs Y parses to a decision-mode scope containing exactly the two options X and Y.
AC2: /priority and /today invoke the hub query and render the returned findings as a numbered list in the hub's ranked order.
AC3: The per-question scope is derived from the command text only and is never persisted.

features.title: Inline-keyboard approve/adjust/reject gate
features.description: Pending approval checkpoints surface in Telegram with an inline keyboard [Approve][Adjust][Reject]. Button taps write the same .approved.json / .rejected.json disk markers used by the existing approve/reject CLI, routing calendar plans and do-bridge promotions through the one existing gate. Adjust writes an approved marker carrying editDelta (steer).
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: Approve writes .bober/approvals/<id>.approved.json matching the ApprovedMarker shape with an approverId.
AC2: Reject writes .bober/approvals/<id>.rejected.json with the supplied feedback; Adjust writes an approved marker whose editDelta carries the supplied text.
AC3: A tap for a checkpoint with no matching .pending.json marker writes no marker file.
AC4: Only whitelisted users' button callbacks are honored.

features.title: Document upload → medical ingest with per-upload opt-in
features.description: Document uploads require an explicit per-upload Yes/No confirmation before anything is processed, because Telegram is not end-to-end encrypted. Only on explicit Yes does the bot download the file and hand it to the existing src/medical ingest path; otherwise the file is discarded. Replies carry a non-sensitive summary only.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: An uploaded document with no confirmation never reaches the medical ingest path.
AC2: After an explicit Yes, the medical ingest path is invoked exactly once with the downloaded file.
AC3: After No (or no confirmation), the file is dropped and nothing is ingested.
AC4: Post-ingest replies contain only a count/summary, never raw marker values or names.

features.title: Streaming run progress + silent scheduled digest delivery
features.description: Two outbound delivery modes over the funnel: (a) streaming — a long-running operation edits one status message in place as progress events arrive, ending with a final summary; (b) silent digest — a scheduler-handed digest payload is sent with notifications disabled. No run/fleet/scheduler logic is added here.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: A sequence of progress updates edits a single message id multiple times rather than posting a new message per tick.
AC2: A digest send sets the disable_notification flag.
AC3: Both modes route through the sendSafe funnel and carry summaries only.

features.title: Multi-LLM "secretary" fleet view (/fleet command + per-agent streaming sections)
features.description: A read-only presentation view that surfaces what each LLM/agent produced in the most recent fleet run. It reads the head's collected bundle .bober/fleet-synthesis.json (the SynthesisBundle { rounds, childResults, findings } from src/fleet/synthesis.ts:29), groups findings by FactRecord.subject (the per-agent childFolder label, src/fleet/shared-blackboard.ts:74-89), and renders one labeled section per agent showing a one-line finding summary, round, confidence, and that agent's finding count. Two surfaces share one renderer: an on-demand /fleet command and the feat-8 streaming/digest path. All output passes through sendSafe — raw findings[].value payloads are never emitted verbatim. No run/fleet/scheduler logic is added; this stays a thin read+render adapter. Validated by research-20260628-telegram-features-and-topology.md (Tier 1) and research-20260628-telegram-multi-llm-coordination-research.md.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: /fleet from a whitelisted user reads .bober/fleet-synthesis.json and replies with one section per distinct findings[].subject, each showing the agent label, a one-line summary, the round, the confidence, and that agent's finding count.
AC2: When .bober/fleet-synthesis.json is missing or has zero findings, /fleet replies with a non-error 'no recent fleet run' message and throws nothing.
AC3: The reply header shows the run's round count (SynthesisBundle.rounds).
AC4: All /fleet and fleet-streaming/digest output passes through sendSafe; a finding's raw value is summarized/truncated to one line and never sent verbatim.
AC5: A non-whitelisted user's /fleet is denied with the numeric-id echo (per feat-2) and triggers no file-read side effect.

assumptions: Integration mechanism: where a sibling module exports a stable function (e.g. the disk-marker helpers in src/state/approval-state.ts:24-80 — savePending/listPending/readPending), the adapter imports it directly; where the surface is a CLI still being finalized by a sibling spec (task inbox, priority hub), the adapter shells out via execa (already a dependency per package.json). Evidence: approval-state.ts exports typed marker helpers; execa is in package.json deps.

Telegram library is a generator decision (per the orchestrator brief): a maintained Node Telegram Bot API library supporting long-polling getUpdates (grammY / telegraf / node-telegram-bot-api). None is present in package.json today, so exactly one new dependency is introduced — only in Sprint 1. Evidence: package.json deps list contains no Telegram library.

Credentials come from the environment, mirroring the existing pattern at src/medical/whoop/whoop-token.ts:47-48: TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (bot token) and TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS (comma-separated numeric ids). No tokens or ids are hardcoded.

Inline approve/adjust/reject resolves into the existing gate by writing the identical disk markers the approve/reject CLI writes (.approved.json / .rejected.json), per src/state/approval-state.ts:24-43 and src/cli/commands/approve.ts. No new approval mechanism is created.

The control-plane privacy rule is enforced in code via a single outbound sendSafe funnel that handlers cannot bypass, plus a mandatory per-upload opt-in before any document reaches medical ingest. Evidence: research §3a lines 140-141 (bot is control-plane + non-sensitive summaries only; lab-PDF-via-Telegram = explicit per-upload opt-in).
The task inbox command surface is `agent-bober task add "..."` (zero-required-field capture) per research §3a line 129-133.

The bot is a thin adapter over the hub/inbox/calendar/scheduler/medical features; their ranking, storage, dedup, slot-filling, parsing, and scheduling logic stays in the sibling modules and is out of scope here.

The multi-LLM 'secretary' view (feat-9) reads the existing head-written artifact .bober/fleet-synthesis.json (the SynthesisBundle shaped by src/fleet/synthesis.ts:29-39: { rounds, childResults, findings }) and groups findings by FactRecord.subject (the per-agent childFolder set in src/fleet/shared-blackboard.ts:74-89). It imports SynthesisBundle/FactRecord as TYPE-ONLY (no runtime coupling to better-sqlite3), adds NO npm dependency, and is the validated Tier-1 topology: coordination already lives in the fleet blackboard, so Telegram only presents it. Evidence: research-20260628-telegram-features-and-topology.md §2-§4; research-20260628-telegram-multi-llm-coordination-research.md (Architecture Overview, Integration Points).
outOfScope: The priority hub ranking / relevance-filter / lens-panel judging logic (owned by spec-20260628-priority-hub).
The task inbox storage, dedup, reconcile, triage, and lifecycle logic (owned by spec-20260628-task-inbox).
The calendar planner slot-filling and free/busy logic (owned by spec-20260628-calendar-planner).
The scheduler / cron that decides digest cadence (this adapter only delivers a handed digest payload silently).
Medical PDF parsing, FactStore writes, and medical guardrails (owned by src/medical).
Webhooks, hosting a public HTTPS endpoint, or any inbound port.
Bot-to-bot autonomous chaining and multi-user fan-out beyond the static whitelist.

DEFERRED (Tier 2, sibling spec): per-LLM Telegram bot identities + a dispatcher/secretary bot messaging worker bots via Bot API 10.0 (May 8 2026) bot-to-bot communication. The capability is real but gated by mutual per-bot opt-in, costs N tokens, and duplicates the existing fleet blackboard bus; pursue only if each LLM must be a distinct addressable Telegram identity. Evidence: research-20260628-telegram-features-and-topology.md §1 (Bot-to-bot), §3 Tier 2.

DEFERRED (Tier 3, own consent-design spec): Telegram Secretary Mode (a bot acting on the user's OWN account to ingest real incoming DMs). It turns the bot into a reader of the user's human chats — a large egress/privacy expansion that tensions this spec's control-plane boundary — and is capped at one business bot per account. Evidence: research-20260628-telegram-features-and-topology.md §1 (Secretary Mode), §3 Tier 3.
Carrying raw PHI or financial detail over Telegram in either direction beyond an explicitly opted-in document upload.

clarificationQuestions.question: Should the adapter invoke sibling features (hub/inbox/medical) by importing their modules directly or by shelling out to the agent-bober CLI?

options.label: A
options.description: Import sibling modules directly for typed, in-process calls.

options.label: B
options.description: Shell out to `agent-bober <subcommand>` via execa for loose coupling.

options.label: C
options.description: Hybrid: import where a stable export exists, shell out otherwise.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: C — import src/state/approval-state.ts helpers directly (stable, typed); shell out via execa for task/hub CLIs still being finalized by sibling specs.

clarificationQuestions.question: Which Node Telegram Bot API library should the long-polling bot use?

options.label: A
options.description: grammY — modern, TypeScript-first, long-polling support.

options.label: B
options.description: telegraf — widely used, mature.

options.label: C
options.description: node-telegram-bot-api — minimal, getUpdates built in.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: Generator's choice among maintained libraries that support getUpdates long-polling; document the pick in generatorNotes. This is the only new dependency, added in Sprint 1.

clarificationQuestions.question: How does the whitelist behave for non-listed users, and where do credentials come from?

options.label: A
options.description: Silently ignore non-listed users.

options.label: B
options.description: Deny with an access-denied reply that echoes the sender's numeric id; credentials from env.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: B — deny + echo id (so the operator can add the id to the whitelist); TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN + TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS from process.env, mirroring src/medical/whoop/whoop-token.ts:47-48.

clarificationQuestions.question: How should the inline approve/adjust/reject buttons resolve into the existing approve/steer gate?

options.label: A
options.description: Add a new Telegram-specific approval store.

options.label: B
options.description: Write the same .approved.json / .rejected.json disk markers the existing CLI writes.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: B — reuse src/state/approval-state.ts markers (PendingMarker/ApprovedMarker/RejectedMarker), with Adjust writing an approved marker carrying editDelta. No new mechanism.

clarificationQuestions.question: How is the control-plane privacy rule enforced in code rather than by convention, given Telegram bot messages are not end-to-end encrypted?

options.label: A
options.description: Document the rule and trust handlers to follow it.

options.label: B
options.description: A single outbound sendSafe funnel all replies pass through, plus a mandatory per-upload opt-in before any document reaches medical ingest.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: B — code-enforced chokepoint (summaries/titles only) plus explicit per-upload opt-in for documents, per research §3a lines 140-141.

clarificationQuestions.question: How should the multi-LLM secretary view surface in Telegram?

options.label: A
options.description: On-demand /fleet command only.

options.label: B
options.description: Both: a /fleet command AND the feat-8 streaming/digest path reuse the per-agent renderer.

options.label: C
options.description: Auto-stream/digest only, no command.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: B — one shared per-agent renderer behind both an on-demand /fleet command and the feat-8 streaming/digest path.

clarificationQuestions.question: Where should the secretary view read fleet state from?

options.label: A
options.description: The .bober/fleet-synthesis.json artifact (head's collected SynthesisBundle) — file read, no DB coupling.

options.label: B
options.description: Both: live `agent-bober blackboard read` (execa) mid-run, JSON artifact after.

options.label: C
options.description: Live blackboard CLI only.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: A — read .bober/fleet-synthesis.json; keeps the adapter thin and avoids coupling src/telegram to better-sqlite3.

clarificationQuestions.question: What should each agent's section show, given sendSafe means summaries only?

options.label: A
options.description: Agent label + one-line summary + round.

options.label: B
options.description: Above + confidence score + finding count.

options.label: C
options.description: Head's merged synthesis first, then per-agent breakdown.

clarificationQuestions.recommendation: B — label + one-line summary + round + confidence + count, all truncated/summarized through sendSafe.

clarificationQuestions.question: Is the design approved to proceed to sprint decomposition autonomously?
clarificationQuestions.recommendation: Autonomous self-approval — all blocking questions self-answered from research doc + substrate evidence; ambiguityScore 4 < 7.

resolvedClarifications.answer: Hybrid (Option C): import src/state/approval-state.ts disk-marker helpers directly (stable typed exports, src/state/approval-state.ts:24-80); shell out via execa (already in package.json deps) to `agent-bober task ...` / hub commands whose surface is finalized by sibling specs spec-20260628-task-inbox and spec-20260628-priority-hub.

Deferred to the generator (per orchestrator brief): pick one maintained library that supports getUpdates long-polling (grammY / telegraf / node-telegram-bot-api) and record the choice in generatorNotes. package.json currently has no Telegram library, so exactly one new dependency is added, only in Sprint 1.

Option B: non-listed senders get an access-denied reply that echoes their exact numeric id and trigger no side effects; listed senders are admitted. Credentials read from process.env TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS (comma-separated), mirroring src/medical/whoop/whoop-token.ts:47-48. Matches research §3a line 139.

Option B: button taps write the identical .approved.json / .rejected.json markers the existing approve/reject CLI writes (src/state/approval-state.ts:24-43; src/cli/commands/approve.ts). Adjust writes an approved marker whose editDelta carries the replacement text (steer). No Telegram-specific approval store.

Option B: a single sendSafe outbound funnel is the only path to the Telegram send transport (handlers return content, the funnel sends), and document uploads require an explicit per-upload Yes/No opt-in before any file reaches medical ingest. Enforces research §3a lines 140-141 (control plane + summaries only; per-upload opt-in for lab PDFs).

Autonomous self-approval. Evidence: research-20260627-knowledge-platform-landscape.md §3a (lines 137-142); src/state/approval-state.ts:24-43; src/cli/commands/medical.ts; src/cli/index.ts:1-41; src/medical/whoop/whoop-token.ts:47-48; package.json deps. Final ambiguityScore 4 < 7, no open questions remain.
Option B: one shared per-agent renderer behind BOTH an on-demand /fleet command and the feat-8 streaming/digest path.
Option A: read the .bober/fleet-synthesis.json artifact (the head's collected SynthesisBundle); no live blackboard CLI, no better-sqlite3 coupling in src/telegram.
Option B: each agent section shows label + one-line summary + round + confidence + finding count, all truncated/summarized through sendSafe.

Autonomous self-approval for the feat-9 enrichment (spec version 2). Every feat-9 assumption is backed by cited evidence: research-20260628-telegram-features-and-topology.md (validated Tier-1 topology, Bot API 10.0 bot-to-bot + Secretary Mode constraints, 13/25 claims confirmed by 3-vote verification); research-20260628-telegram-multi-llm-coordination-research.md; src/fleet/synthesis.ts:15-39 (SynthesisBundle shape); src/fleet/shared-blackboard.ts:13-18,74-105 (BlackboardFinding/readAll/subject grouping); src/state/facts.ts:36 (FactRecord). The three load-bearing UX/integration choices were answered by the user (Q6/Q7/Q8). ambiguityScore 4 < 7; Tier 2/3 explicitly deferred to sibling specs in outOfScope.

techStack: Telegram Bot API — long-polling getUpdates (library chosen by generator)

integrationPoints: spec-20260628-calendar-planner (calendar plan checkpoints surfaced via the approve gate)
Telegram Bot API getUpdates (no server, no public URL, no inbound port)
.bober/fleet-synthesis.json (read-only by feat-9; the head-written SynthesisBundle from src/fleet/synthesis.ts:29) — grouped per-agent by FactRecord.subject for the multi-LLM secretary view
techNotes.dataModel: No new persistent data model. The adapter reads existing .bober/approvals/*.pending.json markers and writes .approved.json/.rejected.json; it forwards task text and document files to sibling features. Ephemeral per-question prioritization scope is parsed from command text and not stored.
securityConsiderations: User-id whitelist (TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS) gates every update and callback; non-listed senders are denied and cause no side effects.
Control-plane outbound funnel: summaries/titles/notifications only — never raw PHI or financial detail.
Mandatory explicit per-upload opt-in before any document reaches medical ingest (Telegram is not end-to-end encrypted).
Credentials from environment only; no tokens/ids in source.

nonFunctionalRequirements.requirement: The bot uses long-polling getUpdates exclusively — no webhook, no inbound listening port, no public HTTPS URL.
nonFunctionalRequirements.verificationMethod: Code review + grep confirms no server/listen/createServer/webhook call; manual run shows getUpdates polling.

nonFunctionalRequirements.requirement: Every update and inline-button callback is gated by the TELEGRAM_ALLOWED_USERS whitelist before any side effect.
nonFunctionalRequirements.verificationMethod: Unit test: non-listed sender id is denied and produces zero side effects.

nonFunctionalRequirements.requirement: Outbound messages carry only non-sensitive summaries; all replies pass through the single sendSafe funnel.
nonFunctionalRequirements.verificationMethod: Unit test + code review: handlers never call the Telegram send transport directly.

nonFunctionalRequirements.requirement: The module is a thin adapter: no ranking, storage, slot-filling, parsing, or scheduling logic is reimplemented here.
nonFunctionalRequirements.verificationMethod: Code review against the outOfScope list; sibling features are invoked, not reimplemented.

constraints: ESM only; all relative imports use .js extensions for NodeNext resolution.
TypeScript strict mode; zero type errors and clean tsc build are hard gates.
consistent-type-imports: use `import type` for type-only imports; no `any` without justification.
No synchronous filesystem ops — use node:fs/promises.
At most one new npm dependency (the Telegram Bot API library), added in Sprint 1 only.
No more than maxSprints (10) sprints; sprintSize medium.
